summary: Spolsky uses a personal anecdote about tracking down a neighbor's malfunctioning clock radio to illustrate a broader point about software quality. The clock radio's poor UI defaults—resetting its alarm to midnight after a power outage—caused a week of lost sleep, a problem that a single better design decision would have prevented. He connects this to the mindset required in the final debugging phase of software development, where developers become hypercritical and hunt for every small flaw. Quoting Dave Winer, he argues that great commercial software is built by fighting for every inch—thousands of tiny improvements that individually seem insignificant but collectively create a product that feels magical. The post concludes by celebrating the FogBugz 6.0 team's beta release as an example of this inch-by-inch craftsmanship.
key_points: Poor default settings in everyday products (like a clock radio alarming at midnight) cause real user pain and represent exactly the kind of small design failure that compounds
Building great software requires a mindset of constant, obsessive criticism—finding fault with everything until every small flaw is addressed
Commercial software quality is the accumulation of thousands of tiny improvements, not a few big features or lucky breaks
Good UI design means anticipating how real users (like an elderly neighbor) will interact with a product and choosing sensible defaults
The difference between decent software and great software is whether even the obscure, rarely-visited corners of the product are polished
money_quotes: Commercial software—the kind you sell to other people—is a game of inches.
To create a usable piece of software, you have to fight for every fix, every feature, every little accommodation that will get one more person up the curve. There are no shortcuts.

Every day you make a tiny bit of progress. You make one thing just a smidgen better. You make the alarm clock default to 7:00am instead of 12:00 midnight. A tiny improvement that will barely benefit anyone. One inch.
It takes a mindset of constant criticism to find them. You have to reshape your mind until you're finding fault with everything.
The inches add up to feet, the feet add up to yards, and the yards add up to miles. And you ship a truly great product.
Even the janitor's closets of your software have marble floors and solid core oak doors and polished mahogany wainscoting.
The UI was terrible. Your average little old lady didn't stand a chance.
And there's no reason to reset the alarm time to midnight after a power outage. 7:00 am would be a completely civilized default.
key_insight: Great software is not built through grand strokes but through the relentless accumulation of thousands of tiny improvements, each one fighting for another inch of quality.
